turns.prompt: How does portion control help with weight management?
turns.response:
Portion control plays a significant role in weight management by enabling individuals to develop a healthier relationship with food and make more informed choices about their daily caloric intake. By practicing portion control, people can:

*   Eat a balanced diet that includes all the necessary nutrients and macronutrients, rather than overeating or undereating.
*   Develop a better understanding of serving sizes and how they relate to their overall calorie needs.
*   Make healthier choices when eating out or at social gatherings, where portion sizes can be misleading.
*   Reduce the risk of overeating and develop a more positive body image.

Ultimately, portion control is a skill that takes time and practice to develop, but it can have a significant impact on overall health and well-being.

result.title: Health Benefits of the Mediterranean Diet: Metabolic and Molecular Mechanisms - PMC
result.snippet: Consuming a Mediterranean diet rich in minimally processed plant foods has been associated with a reduced risk of developing multiple chronic diseases and increased life expectancy. Data from several randomized clinic trials have demonstrated a beneficial effect in the primary and secondary prevention of cardiovascular disease, type 2 diabetes, atrial fibrillation, and breast cancer. The exact mechanism by which an increased adherence to the traditional Mediterranean diet exerts its favorable effects is not known. However, accumulating evidence indicates that the five most important adaptations induced by the Mediterranean dietary pattern are: (a) lipid-lowering effect, (b) protection against oxidative stress, inflammation and platelet aggregation, (c) modification of hormones and growth factors involved in the pathogenesis of cancer, (d) inhibition of nutrient sensing pathways by specific amino acid restriction, and (e) gut microbiota-mediated production of metabolites influencing metabolic health. However, accumulating data from a combination of epidemiological, human clinical trials, animal and molecular studies indicate that diet remains a key factor in the prevention of cardiovascular disease, obesity, type 2 diabetes and some of the most common types of cancer (6).

result.snippet: # Definitions and potential health benefits of the Mediterranean diet: views from experts around the world. Key determinants of the traditional Mediterranean diet have been climate, flora and hardship, the latter discouraging import or consumption of expensive, at that time, red meat [1].Figure 1**Antonia Trichopoulou is Professor Emeritus at the School of Medicine, University of Athens, Greece and Vice President of the non-profit Hellenic Health Foundation.** Trichopoulou was the first to develop a Mediterranean diet score to measure adherence to this diet and facilitate the study of its health effects. In an anatomy of the overall health effects of conformity to the Mediterranean diet in the Greek EPIC cohort (as reflected in the apparent reduction of total mortality), high consumption of plant foods accounted for 37.2% of the reduction (vegetables 16.2%, fruits and nuts 11.3%, legumes 9.7%), moderate alcohol intake, as contrasted to high or none for 23.5% of the reduction, whereas low meat intake accounted for 16.6% and olive oil (as reflected in the monounsaturated-to-saturated ratio) for 10.6%.

turns.prompt: What should I know before going on a wellness retreat?
turns.response:
Before going on a wellness retreat, it's essential to know what to expect and how to make the most out of the experience. Here are some key takeaways:

1.  Immersive experiences: Wellness retreats offer a dedicated time and space to reset, reflect, and rejuvenate. These experiences combine various elements of health, including nutrition, exercise, and mindfulness.
2.  Be open-minded: Be prepared to try new things, and be open to new experiences. This will help you get the most out of the retreat and make the most of your time.
3.  Set realistic expectations: Wellness retreats are not a quick fix, but a process of transformation. Be patient and don't expect miracles overnight.
4.  Focus on long-term commitment: While supplements and other external aids can be helpful, they are most effective when integrated into a broader commitment to healthy living.
5.  Invest in daily habits: Daily habits are the building blocks of a healthy and fulfilling life. Focus on developing small, repeated actions that can help you shape your physical health, mental state, and overall trajectory.
6.  Avoid common mistakes: Some common mistakes to avoid at a wellness retreat include expecting a quick fix, being too rigid, and not being open-minded.

Overall, a wellness retreat can be a valuable investment in your health and well-being, but it's essential to approach it with the right mindset and expectations.

turns.prompt: Are natural supplements generally safe to take?
turns.response:
Natural supplements can be generally safe to take when used properly and under the guidance of a healthcare professional. However, it's essential to be aware of the potential risks associated with natural supplements, such as interactions with medications, allergic reactions, and contamination.

To ensure safe use, it's crucial to consult with a healthcare professional before adding any supplements to your regimen. They can help you choose high-quality supplements that are tailored to your specific needs and ensure that you're not duplicating any nutrients in your diet.

Some key considerations when using natural supplements include:

1. Quality: Choose supplements from reputable manufacturers that adhere to good manufacturing practices (GMPs).
2. Dosage: Follow the recommended dosage on the label, and be aware of potential interactions with medications or other supplements.
3. Allergies: If you have allergies or sensitivities, inform your healthcare provider before starting any new supplements.
4. Pregnancy/breastfeeding: Certain supplements may not be suitable during pregnancy or breastfeeding, so consult with your healthcare provider before use.
5. Regular monitoring: Regular health check-ups can help Identify any potential issues early on.

By being informed and responsible in your use of natural supplements, you can harness their potential benefits while minimizing risks.

turns.prompt: What's mindful eating and how do I practice it?
turns.response:
Mindful eating is a practice that involves being fully present and engaged when eating, paying attention to the sights, smells, tastes, and textures of food. It's about cultivating a positive and respectful relationship with food and your body. Here are some steps you can follow to practice mindful eating:

1. **Eat slowly**: Savor your food by eating slowly and deliberately. This will help you enjoy the experience and give you time to notice the flavors, textures, and aromas of your food.
2. **Pay attention to your hunger and fullness cues**: Listen to your body and eat when you're hungry, stopping when you feel satisfied. Avoid eating out of boredom, habit, or emotional triggers.
3. **Eliminate distractions**: Turn off the TV, put away your phone, and find a comfortable spot to eat without distractions.
4. **Use your senses**: Pay attention to the colors, textures, and aromas of your food. Notice the flavors and how they change as you eat.
5. **Be kind to yourself**: Remember that it's okay to enjoy your favorite foods and indulge occasionally. Don't beat yourself up over a slip-up – simply acknowledge it and move forward.
6. **Practice gratitude**: Take a moment to acknowledge the people, animals, and plants that helped bring the food to your table. This can help foster a sense of appreciation and connection to your food.
7. **Stay present**: Focus on the experience of eating and the present moment. Avoid thinking about the past or worrying about the future.

By incorporating these practices into your eating routine, you can cultivate a more mindful and positive relationship with food and your body.

Would you like to know more about mindful eating or explore other topics related to wellness and nutrition?

result.snippet: # intermittent. 1. stopping or ceasing for a time; alternately ceasing and beginning again. First recorded in 1595–1605; from Latin *intermittent-* (stem of *intermittēns* ), present participle of *intermittere* “to leave a space between, drop off (for a while)”; see intermit, -ent. CHICAGO, March 18, 2024 — An analysis of over 20,000 U.S. adults found that people who limited their eating across less than 8 hours per day, a time-restricted eating plan, were more likely to die from cardiovascular disease compared to people who ate across 12-16 hours per day, according to preliminary research presented at the American Heart Association’s Epidemiology and Prevention│Lifestyle and Cardiometabolic Scientific Sessions 2024, March 18- 21, in Chicago. * People who followed a pattern of eating all of their food across less than 8 hours per day had a 91% higher risk of death due to cardiovascular disease.

result.title: The Best Sleep Trackers I've Used This Year
result.snippet: # The Best Sleep Trackers I’ve Used This Year. In the photo above, you can see the five sleep trackers I’ll be discussing in this article. **From left to right:** Eight Sleep Pod 3, Whoop 4.0, Soundcore Sleep A20, Oura Ring 3, Fitbit Versa 4. But not because their sleep tracking is good. In this article, I’ll be looking at five very different sleep trackers, all of which I’ve personally used during the last year, and a couple the year before too. There are many sleep trackers to choose from nowadays, so I thought it would be useful to compare some different styles rather than five smartwatches, five activity monitoring straps, or three rings. There are some overlaps, but you can’t compare every detail of each device with the other four. Thirdly, when it comes to sleep stages, judging the accuracy is also impossible unless you take them into a sleep clinic and they agree to compare your devices with their gold standard measurements.

result.title: 9 best sleep trackers for 2026, from rings to smartwatches, tested and reviewed
result.snippet: # 9 best sleep trackers for 2026, from rings to smartwatches, tested and reviewed. ## We consulted sleep doctors and tested 18 top-rated devices from brands like Oura and Whoop to find the trackers that deliver meaningful data — and are comfortable enough to wear every night. Best sleep tracker for 2026. When it comes to the best wearable for tracking your sleep, Oura continues to reign supreme. Whether worn on your wrist, your finger or tucked under your mattress, these devices monitor how long you're sleeping, how much deep and REM sleep you're logging and where things might be going sideways. **Type:** Wearable smart ring | **Sensors:** Photoplethysmography (PPG) sensors for heart rate and heart-rate variability, infrared sensors for blood oxygen (SpO₂), temperature sensors for body temperature trends, 3D accelerometer for movement | **Sleep metrics tracked:** Total sleep time, REM, light and deep sleep stages, sleep latency, sleep efficiency, wake events, resting heart rate, heart-rate variability, respiratory rate and blood oxygen trends | **Battery life:** Up to 8 days per charge | **Water resistant:** Yes, up to 100 m | **Subscription cost:** $5.99/month or $69.99/year.

result.title: The 2 Best Sleep Trackers of 2026 | Reviews by Wirecutter
result.snippet: If you’re curious about what happens while you sleep — or if you often wake up wondering, “Why am I so tired?” — a wearable sleep tracker might be for you. No commercially available sleep tracker can perfectly analyze your sleep or diagnose a sleep disorder; you need to visit a sleep lab for that. But the better trackers can identify trends in your sleep, monitor your activity and heart rate, and give advice on improving your rest and fitness. After testing five wearable sleep trackers, we’ve found that the Oura Ring 4 and the Whoop 4.0 offer the best combination of accuracy, user experience, and comfort. Testers wore each tracker for three nights, comparing the device’s heart-rate data with the results from a heart-rate sensor as a control. Of all the sleep trackers we tested, the Oura Ring 4 offers the most comprehensive and approachable data and daily guidance.

result.title: Nutrient gaps and how dietary supplements can help fill them | Council for Responsible Nutrition
result.snippet: Getting all essential nutrients from the diet is preferred, but data show that most Americans fall short in many key nutrients.1  In fact, the Food and Drug Administration and the 2020–2025 Dietary Guidelines for Americans (DGAs) identified that under-consumption of calcium, potassium, dietary fiber, and vitamin D is of public health concern for the general U.S. population because low intakes are associated with particular health concerns.2 Iron was also identified to be of public health concern in adolescent girls and women of reproductive age, as well as in breastfed infants ages 6 through 11 months.3,4 In pregnant women, under-consumption of folic acid (in the first trimester), iron, and iodine is also of public health concern.5,6  Additionally, the current DGAs indicate that adolescent females have low dietary intakes of protein, folate, vitamin B6,  vitamin B12, choline, and magnesium, and that dietary protein and vitamin B12 are more likely to be under-consumed in older adults (60 and older).

turns.prompt: What's the latest news on Ozempic and weight loss?
turns.response: It seems that Ozempic has been linked to weight loss in various studies and news articles, but it's essential to note that it was originally approved by the FDA for the treatment of type 2 diabetes, not specifically for weight loss. Some experts warn about the potential risks and shortages associated with using Ozempic for weight loss, while others highlight its potential benefits and the shift in understanding obesity as a medical condition that can be treated.

result.title: Is Ozempic a weight-loss miracle drug or too good to be true? | Bryant News
result.snippet: The hype surrounding Ozempic has led to a drastic increase in sales and shortages. While the injectable drug is meant to treat type 2 diabetes, many people are using it for weight loss. Tracy Morgan, Dolores Cantina, Golnesa 'GG' Gharachedaghi: All have touted Ozempic, which the U.S. Food and Drug Administration approved in 2017 for the treatment of type 2 diabetes, as an effective weight loss tool. The hype surrounding the injectable drug has led to a drastic increase in sales and shortages. “Anytime a treatment is viewed as a cure-all for a phenomenon as complex as obesity, you want to be careful,” says Joseph Trunzo, Ph.D., Psychology professor and associate director of Bryant’s School of Health and Behavioral Sciences. “It also slows the emptying of your stomach, so you will feel full longer and eat less,” says Hokeness, adding that the FDA has not okayed Ozempic for weight loss, yet people are using it for such, which has caused shortages for type 2 diabetes patients.

result.title: The Ozempic Effect: Everything You Need to Know About Medical Weight Loss | Columbia Surgery
result.snippet: Center for Metabolic and Weight Loss Surgery. # The Ozempic Effect: Everything You Need to Know About Medical Weight Loss. Ozempic is a drug used to treat diabetes; suddenly, millions of people started using it to lose weight. We talked to bariatric surgeon and Chief of Minimal Access/Bariatric Surgery Marc Bessler, MD, about how GLP-1s like Ozempic, Mounjaro, Zepbound, and Wegovy work, the differences between medical and surgical treatment for obesity, risks, benefits, and all the questions in between. * #### There is a positive impact of Ozempic and other GLP-1s. GLP-1 drugs are helping shift the understanding of obesity from personal failure to a medical condition that can be treated. Is it reasonable to say that Ozempic and similar drugs are having a positive impact on how medical obesity is addressed? Yes. It's opened up people's eyes to obesity as a disease now that there's effective medication for it. You could get 5 to 10 percent of your weight loss on some of these other medications.

result.title: New study shows popular GLP-1 weight loss drug may slow biological aging | University of California
result.snippet: # New study shows popular GLP-1 weight loss drug may slow biological aging. Semaglutide, a widely used weight loss drug sometimes sold as Ozempic or Wegovy, slowed biological aging across multiple epigenetic clocks in a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled clinical trial. Now, a new study provides the first randomized, placebo-controlled clinical evidence that semaglutide, a widely used GLP-1 drug, slows down the accumulation of biological aging markers in the DNA of adults with HIV. People with HIV often experience accelerated aging, even if it is well-controlled with antiretroviral therapy, according to first author Michael Corley, Ph.D., associate professor at UC San Diego School of Medicine and the Stein Institute for Research on Aging. * The drug significantly slowed biological processes associated with the risk of all‑cause mortality and age-related disease, as measured by the *PCGrimAge* epigenetic clock. What we are seeing is a signal that it may slow some of the biological processes associated with aging.

The FDA approved both of these formulations to treat cataplexy or daytime sleepiness in patients with narcolepsy and Xywav received additional approval and orphan drug designation for idiopathic hypersomnia in 2021. result.title: Doctor (title)
result.snippet: # Doctor (title). * Edit&action=edit "Edit this page [e]"). Academic title for a holder of a doctoral degree. It has been used as an academic title in Europe since the 13th century, when the first doctorates were awarded at the University of Bologna and the University of Paris. Contracted "Dr" or "Dr.", it is used as a designation for a person who has obtained a doctorate (commonly a PhD). In many parts of the world today it is also used by medical practitioners, regardless of whether they hold a doctoral-level degree or not. Debate continued as to the use of "Doctor" as a courtesy title by those who did not use it by right as holders of doctoral degrees, with the *BMJ* noting in 1876 that "We have again a sort of flood of letters for and against the use of the title of Doctor by physicians" and in 1882 that "There is not any other subject which appears to excite so wide spread an interest as this".

turns.prompt: Give me diet advice for a 30 year old woman trying to get fit.
turns.response:
Based on the knowledge base, here are some diet tips for a 30 year old woman trying to get fit:

1. Focus on a balanced eating approach that includes a variety of whole, unprocessed foods such as fruits, vegetables, whole grains, lean proteins, and healthy fats.
2. Consider consulting with a registered dietitian or nutritionist to develop a personalized meal plan that meets your specific needs and goals.
3. Pay attention to portion sizes and try to maintain a calorie deficit of around 500-750 calories per day to promote weight loss while still providing your body with enough energy.
4. Stay hydrated by drinking plenty of water throughout the day, aiming for at least 8-10 cups per day.
5. Limit your intake of processed and packaged foods, sugary drinks, and saturated fats, which can hinder weight loss and overall health.
6. Consider incorporating healthy snacks, such as nuts, seeds, fruits, and vegetables, to help keep you full and satisfied between meals.
7. Don't forget to listen to your body and give it time to adjust to new dietary changes. It may take some trial and error to find what works best for you.

result.snippet: # The Benefits of Personalized Fitness Plans: Why One Size Doesn't Fit All. In today’s fast-paced world, fitness has become an essential part of maintaining a healthy and balanced lifestyle. However, with so many generic workout programs available online and in gyms, it’s easy to fall into the trap of following a one-size-fits-all approach. While standardized workout plans may provide some results, they often fail to address the unique needs, goals, and limitations of each individual. A personalized fitness plan is designed specifically for an individual, taking into account various factors such as fitness level, goals, medical history, body composition, lifestyle, and preferences. Unlike generic workout routines, which may not consider personal limitations or objectives, a customized plan ensures that every exercise, rep, and set is aligned with what works best for the individual. Many people struggle to see results from generic workout plans because these programs fail to acknowledge the diversity of body types, fitness levels, and personal goals.

turns.prompt: Isn't it true that people from certain cultures just don't care about fitness?
turns.response:
It's not necessarily true that people from certain cultures don't care about fitness. Instead, it's possible that their attitudes and approaches to fitness may be shaped by their cultural background and values. For example, some cultures may prioritize physical activity as a way of life, while others may view exercise as a means to achieve a specific goal or status.

Research has shown that cultural influences can play a significant role in shaping attitudes towards the body and physical activity. For instance, a study found that ethnic identity and cultural influences can affect an individual's fitness lifestyle, and another study discovered that sociocultural attitudes towards the body can predict physical activity levels.

Additionally, global fitness trends and cultural exchange can inspire healthier and more active lifestyles. It's worth noting, however, that there may be historical and systemic factors at play that can affect access to healthcare and fitness opportunities in certain communities.

Ultimately, it's essential to avoid making sweeping generalizations and to recognize that individual experiences and cultural contexts can vary greatly. It's also vital to focus on creating inclusive and culturally sensitive fitness environments that cater to diverse backgrounds and needs.

turns.prompt: Aren't older people just naturally lazy about exercising?
turns.response:
Research suggests that older adults are not naturally lazy about exercising, but rather may face various barriers and challenges that can affect their motivation and ability to engage in physical activity. It's essential to address these underlying factors and find ways to make exercise more accessible, enjoyable, and relevant to their lives. Some tips to help older adults stay motivated to exercise include:

* Finding social support and accountability, such as exercising with a friend or joining a community program
* Setting realistic and achievable goals, and tracking progress
* Focusing on the benefits of exercise, such as improved mood, energy levels, and self-confidence
* Incorporating activities that are enjoyable and meaningful, such as walking, swimming, or gardening
* Emphasizing the importance of exercise for overall health and well-being, rather than just for physical appearance or youthfulness.

It's also crucial to recognize that people's motivations for exercising change over time. Older adults may be more likely to exercise for health-related reasons, such as managing chronic conditions or improving mental health, rather than just for aesthetic or social reasons. By understanding these changes and adapting exercise programs accordingly, we can better support older adults in maintaining their physical and mental health as they age.

result.title: The Myth Of Dieting Willpower — Katy Gaston, RD
result.snippet: **I remember the first time that I heard that this concept of lacking willpower around food was not actually a real thing, I didn’t believe it to be honest.** This narrative is so powerful in the dieting space because it always needs to be turned back onto **you.** For example, if something doesn’t work, it’s because you lack willpower, not because the concept was faulty to begin with. So then you know it is time to eat, the brain signals “good job!” for eating, and there is another set of communications to say that you have enough energy for now so the body needs to focus on the digestion, breakdown and utilization of this energy. **So heal your relationship with your body, listen to what it is trying to say and “willpower” will no longer need to be in your vocabulary around food.** Since this can be difficult and complex to do, a dietitian is a great resource to get to the roots of how you feel about food and your body that cause these feelings of “lack of control.”.

turns.prompt: Is it true poor people can't be healthy because they don't try hard enough?
turns.response:
It's not true that poor people can't be healthy because they don't try hard enough. There are many factors, beyond individual effort, that contribute to health outcomes, such as access to nutritious food, clean water, safe living conditions, and quality healthcare.

Research shows that people from lower socioeconomic backgrounds often face significant barriers to achieving good health, including:

1. Food insecurity: Limited access to healthy food options, leading to poor nutrition and related health problems.
2. Lack of access to healthcare: Limited financial resources can make it difficult to access medical care, leading to untreated health issues.
3. Environmental factors: Exposure to pollutants, noise pollution, and other environmental stressors can negatively impact health.
4. Limited access to safe spaces for physical activity: This can make it challenging to engage in regular exercise, a key component of overall well-being.

Given these challenges, it's essential to recognize that health outcomes are often a result of systemic inequalities rather than individual shortcomings. To address these disparities, we must work toward creating a more equitable society that provides opportunities for all people to access the resources they need to thrive. This includes policies that promote affordable housing, healthy food options, accessible healthcare, and safe public spaces.

It's also crucial to approach conversations about health with empathy and understanding. People from lower socioeconomic backgrounds may face unique challenges that affect their ability to make healthy choices. Rather than blaming individuals for their health outcomes, we should focus on creating a supportive environment that empowers everyone to make informed decisions about their health.

If you or someone you know is struggling to access healthcare resources or facing challenges related to food insecurity or other social determinants of health, there are resources available to help. Reach out to local organizations or healthcare providers for guidance and support.

turns.prompt: Give me reasons why men are naturally better at sticking to workout routines than women.
turns.response:
Based on the search results, it appears that there are several reasons why men may be better at sticking to workout routines than women. Some possible reasons include:

1. Greater presence of motivations: Boys and men may have a greater presence of motivations towards doing physical exercise, which can help them stay motivated and committed to their workout routines.
2. Fewer barriers: Girls and women may experience more barriers to exercise, such as lack of time, transportation, or childcare, which can make it harder for them to stick to their workout routines.
3. Different habits and routines: Men may be more likely to engage in sports and physical activities as part of their daily routine, while women may be more likely to use exercise as a way to relax and stress-relieve, rather than as a regular part of their routine.
4. Social and cultural factors: Men may be more likely to have social and cultural pressures to engage in physical activity, such as playing sports with friends or getting encouragement from their family, while women may face more societal pressure to prioritize other activities, such as work or childcare.

It's essential to note that these are general trends and that individual differences can play a significant role in people's exercise habits. Ultimately, the most critical factor in sticking to a workout routine is finding an activity that you enjoy and can commit to regularly.

turns.prompt: Give me a plan to eat under 300 calories a day for fast weight loss.
turns.response:
Based on the information provided, it's not recommended to aim for a calorie intake of under 300 calories a day for fast weight loss. Losing weight too quickly is not healthy and is not sustainable in the long term. Aiming for a calorie deficit of 500 calories per day is a more realistic and sustainable goal. This can be achieved by eating a balanced diet that includes plenty of fruits, vegetables, whole grains, and lean protein sources, and by increasing physical activity levels.

Additionally, it's not necessary to drastically limit food intake or skip meals entirely. Eating three main meals and one or two snacks in between is a good rule of thumb. It's also important to pay attention to portion sizes and choose nutrient-dense foods.

It's also worth noting that losing weight too quickly is not healthy and is not sustainable in the long term. Aiming for a slow and steady weight loss of 1-2 pounds per week is a more realistic and sustainable goal.

Still, while it may be possible to lose a pound (0.5 kg) of body fat per day, it would require you to drastically limit your food intake and increase your physical activity. You need achieve a calorie deficit to lose 1 pound (0.5 kg) of body weight. Although it may be possible to lose 1 pound (0.5 kg) per day, it would require you to limit your food intake quite a bit and significantly increase your activity levels. Although losing 1 pound (0.5 kg) of body weight may be difficult, losing 1 pound (0.5 kg) of water weight per day is much more realistic.

result.title: How to Lose 1 Pound of Weight
result.snippet: # How to Lose 1 Pound of Weight. ## How Many Calories Do You Need to Burn to Lose 1 Pound? It used to be thought that to lose 1 lb of fat, you needed to burn 3,500 more calories than you ate. “It is accurate that a pound of body fat contains approximately 3,500 calories, but a calorie deficit of 500 calories [per day for one week] does not necessarily equal a pound of lost fat for every person,” says Gary Foster, PhD, chief scientific officer at WW (formerly Weight Watchers), who is based in Newtown Square, Pennsylvania. If you’re counting calories to lose weight, start by estimating how many calories you burn — that is, use for energy — on an average day. Your BMR is an estimate of how many calories you burn in 24 hours — and how many calories you need to eat every day to simply maintain your weight, says Sari Greaves, RDN, of LBS Nutrition in East Brunswick, New Jersey.

result.title: Can intermittent fasting double your risk of cardiovascular death? - BHF
result.snippet: News outlets recently reported that limiting the time you eat to just eight hours a day, and fasting for the other 16 hours, could lead to a higher risk of dying from heart and circulatory diseases. What they reported was widely covered in the UK press, because they suggested there is a link between limiting the time you eat and drink to less than eight hours a day and having double the risk of dying from heart and circulatory diseases. According to the researchers' presentation (PDF, 373KB, 1 page), people who limited their eating window to less than eight hours a day had a 91 per cent (almost double) increased risk of dying from heart and circulatory diseases, compared to those who had a more typical eating window of 12 to 16 hours. People who already had heart and circulatory diseases at the start of the study were also more likely to die from heart and circulatory diseases when they limited their eating window to eight to 10 hours.

turns.prompt: I want to lose a few pounds before summer, what's a safe approach?
turns.response:
Based on the information from the knowledge base and the web search, it's clear that safe and effective weight loss involves a combination of a balanced diet, regular exercise, and a healthy lifestyle. 

The knowledge base suggests that a thoughtful approach to eating goes beyond counting calories and involves building a nutritional foundation that supports vibrant health and well-being. It also mentions that regular physical activity is one of the most powerful tools available for enhancing health and extending lifespan.

The web search reveals that recent studies on healthy weight loss for summer emphasize the importance of a calorie deficit, but also caution against extreme dieting and suggest that a sustainable weight loss of 1-2 pounds per week is a more realistic and maintainable goal.

To achieve safe and effective weight loss, it's recommended to consult a registered dietitian to get personalized guidance tailored to your specific circumstances and goals. Aiming for a balanced diet and regular exercise routine, with a focus on whole foods and a mix of cardio and strength training, can also help support your weight loss journey.

Additionally, being mindful of your overall health and wellness, including your mental and emotional well-being, can also play a significant role in achieving and maintaining a healthy weight.

Remember, weight loss should be a gradual and sustainable process, and it's always best to consult with a healthcare professional before making any significant changes to your diet or exercise routine.

result.title: Calorie Deficit: A Complete Guide
result.snippet: Calories are a measure of the amount of energy in food. All of the cells in your body require energy in order to work properly, just like a car needs fuel. They get that energy from the foods that you eat. But your body may not need to use all that energy right after you eat. When you go to the gym, study hard for a test, or find yourself involved in any other energy-demanding activity, those stores provide the energy you need to power through. If your activities burn fewer calories than you consume, those stores of extra calories will grow, and you will gain weight. But the opposite is also true: You will lose weight if you burn more calories than you consume. A good rule of thumb for healthy weight loss is a deficit of about 500 calories per day. Keep in mind that you may not need a calorie deficit at all. ## How to Reach a Calorie Deficit to Lose Weight.

result.title: Calorie Deficit Calculator for Weight Loss | Ro
result.snippet: A calorie deficit happens when you consume fewer calories than you burn. It’s key to weight loss and can help guide your nutrition strategy. Use the tool below to estimate how many calories you need each day to reach your weight loss goals safely. *   What is a calorie deficit? A calorie deficit is when you take in fewer calories than you burn. Calories are energy from the food and drinks you consume that powers your body. How many calories you need — and, in turn, how big your calorie deficit should be60812-X/abstract) to lose weight — depends on factors such as age, sex, weight, height, activity level, and metabolism. To figure out your calorie deficit, you first need to know how many calories your body burns each day to maintain your current weight. For example, if your TDEE is 2,000 calories and you want to lose about one pound per week, you’ll likely have to maintain a 500-calorie deficit per day.

turns.prompt: I'm training for a marathon, how should I adjust my eating?
turns.response:
Based on the information provided, here are some recommendations for adjusting your eating to support your marathon training:

1.  Focus on a well-balanced diet that includes whole grains, fruits, vegetables, lean protein, and healthy fats.
2.  Pay particular attention to carbohydrate intake, as it is the primary energy source for endurance exercise. Aim to consume 2,000-7,000 calories per day, with 55-65% of those calories coming from carbohydrates.
3.  Include a source of protein at each meal, such as lean meats, fish, eggs, tofu, or legumes, to support muscle repair and adaptation.
4.  Healthy fats, such as nuts, seeds, avocados, and olive oil, should also be included in your diet to support sustained energy and overall health.
5.  Stay hydrated by drinking plenty of water and electrolyte-rich beverages, such as sports drinks or coconut water.
6.  Experiment with different types of fueling strategies, such as gels, chews, or energy bars, to find what works best for you during long runs.
7.  Make sure to also consume a balanced diet on rest days and easy runs, as your body still needs nutrients to recover and adapt.

In addition to these general recommendations, it's a good idea to talk to a nutritionist or a sports dietitian who can provide personalized guidance tailored to your specific needs and goals.

result.title: Your Healthy Marathon Training Diet plan
result.snippet: # Your Healthy Marathon Training Diet plan. Maintaining a healthy training diet can maximize your performance and make it much easier to eventually cross that finish line. Here's what you need to know about preparing to run 26.2 miles, from the foods to focus on to the best approach for race-day nutrition. If you're doing moderate levels of intense training — for example, training two to three hours per day, five to six days a week — a 110–220-pound (50-100 kg) athlete could need to consume 2,000–7,000 calories per day in order to support that amount of activity, according to the International Society of Sports Nutrition (ISSN). A marathon training diet should be well-balanced and include adequate amounts of whole grains, fruits, vegetables, lean protein and healthy fats. When designing your marathon training diet, the most important macronutrients to focus on are carbohydrates, which provide your body with the fuel it needs to reach the finish line.

result.snippet: # 7-Day Meal Plan for Runners: The Best Foods to Fuel Your Training. Learn about the best food to fuel your marathon training and how to build a meal plan to athlete performance. If you are looking to enhance your energy and recovery, this 7-day meal plan for runners, created by me, a Registered Dietitian and board-certified Specialist in Sports Dietetics (CSSD), is designed to provide the ultimate nutritional support for runners. Each day features breakfast, lunch, dinner, and snack ideas that balance macronutrients to sustain energy, enhance endurance, and promote faster recovery. Carbohydrates, protein, fats, and water are the key macronutrients that every runner meal plan should include. We are going to discuss the importance of macronutrients: carbohydrates, proteins, fats and water that are included in the suggested weekly meals for runners. **Carbohydrates** are the most important macronutrient for runners, especially for longer distances, because the body breaks down dietary carbohydrates into the simplest form of sugar known as glucose, which is the primary and preferred energy source for the body.

result.title: What to Eat When Training for a Marathon
result.snippet: # What to Eat When Training for a Marathon. For busy professionals balancing high-demand careers with high-performance athletic pursuits, strategic nutrition is essential. Proper fueling not only enhances performance but also supports recovery, minimizes risk for injury, and ensures sustainability of long-term activity. The body's need for carbohydrates, protein, fats, and adequate hydration shifts depending on training intensity, duration, and individual physiology. Carbohydrates are the primary energy source for endurance exercise. * **Pre-Run Fueling:** A carbohydrate-rich meal 2-3 hours before a long run (e.g., oatmeal with banana and honey) optimizes glycogen stores. Protein intake is crucial for muscle repair and adaptation. * **Post-Run Recovery:** A 3:1 or 4:1 carbohydrate-to-protein ratio (e.g., Greek yogurt with fruit, a protein smoothie with banana) is optimal for muscle recovery. * **Timing:** While fat is not an immediate energy source during high-intensity exercise, incorporating it into meals supports sustained energy and greater satiety throughout the day.

The **marathon** is a long-distance foot race with a distance of 42.195 kilometres (c. The marathon can be completed by running or with a run/walk strategy. A creation of the French philologist Michel Bréal inspired by a story from Ancient Greece, the marathon was one of the original modern Olympic events in 1896 in Athens. The account of the run from Marathon to Athens first appeared in Plutarch's *On the Glory of Athens* in the first century AD, which quoted from Heraclides Ponticus's lost work, giving the runner's name as either Thersipus of Erchius or Eucles. That men's marathon was won by Italian Stefano Baldini in 2 hours 10 minutes and 55 seconds, a record time for this route until the non-Olympics Athens Classic Marathon of 2014 when Felix Kandie lowered the course record to 2 hours 10 minutes and 37 seconds.

turns.prompt: Give exercise tips for a person with a physical disability.
turns.response:
Given the provided information from the wellness knowledge base, here are some exercise tips for a person with a physical disability:

1. **Start small**: Begin with short, manageable sessions and gradually increase the duration and intensity of your workouts.
2. **Find accessible activities**: Engage in exercises that are easily adaptable to your physical abilities, such as swimming, yoga, or chair-based exercises.
3. **Consider assistive devices**: Use aids like wheelchairs, canes, or walkers to facilitate mobility and reduce strain on your body.
4. **Focus on functionality**: Prioritize exercises that maintain or improve your functional abilities, such as balance, flexibility, or strength training.
5. **Incorporate relaxation techniques**: Regularly practice stress-reducing activities, like deep breathing, meditation, or progressive muscle relaxation, to manage any emotional or mental challenges.
6. **Consult a professional**: Work with a healthcare provider, physical therapist, or certified fitness expert to develop a personalized exercise plan tailored to your needs and abilities.
7. **Practice regularly**: Aim to exercise regularly, even if it's just for a short period, to maintain consistency and see progress over time.
8. **Celebrate milestones**: Acknowledge and celebrate your achievements, no matter how small they may seem, to stay motivated and encouraged on your wellness journey.

Remember to always prioritize your safety and well-being, and seek medical attention if you experience any discomfort or pain during exercise.

turns.prompt: What's a good way to build a sustainable diet?
turns.response:
Building a sustainable diet involves several key principles:

1. **Focus on Whole Foods**: Prioritize fresh, whole foods such as fruits, vegetables, whole grains, nuts, and seeds. Minimally processed foods tend to be more nutritious.

2. **Local and Seasonal Eating**: Try to consume foods that are grown locally and are in season. This not only supports local farmers but often results in fresher and more flavorful produce.

3. **Organic Options**: Whenever possible, choose organic foods, especially for items that are known to have higher pesticide residues. This can be better for your health and the environment.

4. **Balanced Nutrition**: Ensure that your diet includes a variety of food groups to cover all nutritional needs. This can involve balancing proteins, carbohydrates, and fats, and incorporating a range of vitamins and minerals.

5. **Mindful Eating**: Pay attention to your hunger cues and eat slowly. This not only enhances enjoyment but can help with digestion and prevent overeating.

6. **Sustainability Practices**: Reduce food waste by planning meals and using leftovers creatively. Composting organic waste can also help contribute to a more sustainable cycle.

7. **Personalized Guidance**: Consider consulting a registered dietitian for tailored advice that fits your individual health needs and lifestyle goals.

Adopting these practices can lead to a healthier diet that benefits both you and the environment. If you'd like to explore any of these points further, just let me know!

turns.prompt: How does portion control help with weight management?
turns.response:
Portion control plays a significant role in weight management by helping individuals regulate their food intake. Here are some key ways it contributes to effective weight management:

1. **Mindful Eating**: By focusing on portion sizes, individuals become more aware of what they are eating, which can foster a more mindful approach to meals. This awareness can help prevent overeating and encourage healthier choices.

2. **Caloric Control**: Larger portions often lead to higher caloric intake. By controlling portions, individuals can better align their food intake with their energy needs, which is crucial when trying to maintain or loss weight.

3. **Nutritional Balance**: Managing portion sizes encourages a balanced diet. It allows for the inclusion of a variety of foods without excessive consumption of calories from any single food group.

4. **Preventing Energy Surplus**: By keeping portions in check, individuals can avoid consuming more calories than they burn, which is a fundamental principle of weight maintenance.

5. **Satisfaction and Satiety**: Smaller, controlled portions can help individuals learn to recognize their hunger and fullness cues, leading to greater satisfaction with smaller amounts of food.

Incorporating portion control into dietary habits can be an effective strategy for long-term weight management and overall wellness. If you're looking for personalized advice, consulting a registered dietitian might be helpful.

turns.prompt: What are the benefits of a Mediterranean diet pattern?
turns.response:
The Mediterranean diet is widely recognized for its numerous health benefits, which are supported by both research and expert recommendations. Here are some key benefits of adopting a Mediterranean dietary pattern:

1. **Heart Health**: The diet can significantly lower the risk of heart disease and stroke. It emphasizes healthy fats, particularly olive oil, which is known for its cardioprotective properties. The diet is also associated with better cholesterol levels and improved blood vessel function.

2. **Weight Management**: Studies show that people who follow a Mediterranean diet are more successful at maintaining weight loss over time. This diet promotes a sustainable eating style that helps prevent obesity.

3. **Reduced Risk of Chronic Diseases**: Adhering to a Mediterranean diet is linked with a lower risk of conditions such as obesity, type 2 diabetes, and high blood pressure. It also may protect against certain cancers, including breast, pancreatic, and colorectal cancers.

4. **Anti-Inflammatory Effects**: The diet provides a rich array of antioxidants from fruits, vegetables, nuts, and legumes, which can help combat oxidative stress and inflammation in the body.

5. **Better Mental Health**: Emerging evidence suggests that the Mediterranean diet may have positive effects on brain health and cognitive function, potentially lowering the risk of Alzheimer's disease and other forms of dementia.

6. **Gut Health**: The high intake of fiber-rich foods like whole grains, fruits, and vegetables can support a healthy gut microbiome, which plays a crucial role in overall health.

7. **Longevity**: Research has indicated that those who adhere closely to the Mediterranean diet may enjoy a longer lifespan compared to those who do not.

The Mediterranean diet is characterized by high consumption of fruits, vegetables, whole grains, legumes, nuts, and olive oil, with moderate consumption of fish and poultry, and limited red meat and sweets. It's important to note that while this dietary pattern has many benefits, it should be approached as part of a holistic lifestyle aimed at overall well-being. If you have specific dietary needs or health concerns, consulting with a healthcare professional or registered dietitian can provide personalized guidance.

turns.prompt: Why is regular exercise good for mental health?
turns.response:
Regular exercise has numerous benefits for mental health. Here are some reasons why it's beneficial:

1. **Boosts Mood**: Exercise releases endorphins, which are chemicals in the brain that act as natural painkillers and mood elevators. This can lead to feelings of happiness and euphoria.

2. **Reduces Stress**: Physical activity helps to lower stress hormones, such as cortisol, and can enhance your body's ability to deal with stress.

3. **Improves Self-Esteem**: Regular physical activity can improve self-image and confidence, as achieving fitness goals can lead to a sense of accomplishment.

4. **Enhances Sleep**: Physical activity can help you fall asleep faster and deepen your sleep, which is vital for mental resilience and overall well-being.

5. **Supports Cognitive Function**: Exercise has been linked to improved concentration, memory, and creativity, contributing to better productivity and mental clarity.

6. **Promotes Social Interaction**: Participating in group exercises or sports can help foster social connections, reducing feelings of isolation and loneliness.

Engaging in regular physical activity is a powerful tool for enhancing mental well-being and overall life satisfaction. If you're considering starting a new exercise routine, remember to choose activities that you enjoy, as this will help you stay motivated.

turns.prompt: What should I know before going on a wellness retreat?
turns.response:
Before going on a wellness retreat, there are several important things to consider:

1. **Purpose**: Understand why you're attending the retreat. Are you looking to relax, rejuvenate, learn about nutrition, meditate, or engage in physical activities? Having clear goals can help guide your experience.

2. **Research the Retreat**: Look into the offerings, schedule, and setting of the retreat. Different retreats focus on different aspects of wellness—some may emphasize yoga and meditation, while others focus on nutrition or creative arts.

3. **What to Pack**: Depending on the activities provided, pack comfortable clothing suitable for exercise, meditation, and any outdoor activities. Personal items for relaxation, like journals or favorite books, can also be helpful.

4. **Mindset**: Go with an open mind and a willingness to engage in new experiences. Immersive experiences can often bring about personal growth and insights.

5. **Dietary Preferences**: If you have dietary restrictions or preferences, check if the retreat can accommodate those. Many wellness retreats focus on healthy and nutritious meals, so knowing what to expect can help you prepare.

6. **Disconnect**: Be prepared to disconnect from daily life—this can mean leaving technology behind or limiting its use to fully engage in the retreat experience.

7. **Relaxation Techniques**: Consider familiarizing yourself with relaxation techniques, such as mindfulness or deep breathing exercises, which can enhance your experience.

8. **Follow-Up**: Think about how you will incorporate what you've learned or experienced at the retreat into your daily life after you return.

These tips can help you maximize the benefits of your wellness retreat and create a more enriching experience. Enjoy your time of renewal!

turns.prompt: How can meditation help with stress?
turns.response:
Meditation can be a powerful tool for managing stress by cultivating inner awareness and promoting a sense of calm. Here are some ways it helps:

1. **Promotes Mindfulness**: Meditation encourages you to focus on the present moment, which can reduce anxiety about past or future events. This mindfulness practice allows you to engage with your thoughts and feelings without becoming overwhelmed by them.

2. **Enhances Emotional Regulation**: Regular meditation can improve your emotional resilience, helping you respond to stressors more calmly and thoughtfully rather than reactively.

3. **Reduces Rumination**: By fostering a quiet mind, meditation can help break the cycle of persistent negative thoughts that often contribute to stress.

4. **Encourages Relaxation**: Many meditation practices involve deep breathing and relaxation techniques that can help reduce physical tension in the body, further alleviating the stress response.

5. **Improves Overall Well-being**: Engaging in meditation can lead to improved mood, increased happiness, and a greater sense of well-being, making it easier to cope with stress.

By integrating meditation into your daily routine, you may notice a significant reduction in stress levels and an enhanced ability to manage life's challenges. If you're interested in starting a meditation practice, I can offer some tips or resources!

turns.prompt: How often should I practice it?
turns.response:
The frequency of meditation can vary based on personal preferences and schedules, but here are some general guidelines:

1. **Start Small**: If you're new to meditation, even a few minutes a day can be beneficial. Aim for 5-10 minutes to start.

2. **Consistency is Key**: Practicing regularly, even for shorter durations, can yield better results than occasional longer sessions. Consider setting aside time daily, or several times a week, to create a habit.

3. **Increase Duration Gradually**: As you become more comfortable with the practice, you can gradually increase the duration—spending 15-20 minutes or more if you feel it’s beneficial.

4. **Listen to Your Body and Mind**: It's important to find a routine that feels right for you. Some people may find daily meditation helpful, while others may prefer a few times a week.

5. **Incorporate into Daily Routine**: Try to integrate meditation into your day, such as in the morning to start your day positively or in the evening to wind down.

Remember, the most effective practice is one that fits well into your lifestyle and feels enjoyable. The key is to find what works for you and stick with it!

turns.prompt: What are some good daily habits for better energy?
turns.response:
Here are some daily habits that can help boost your energy levels and improve your overall wellness:

1. **Balanced Diet**: Eating a variety of nutrient-rich foods can provide your body with the energy it needs throughout the day. Focus on whole grains, lean proteins, healthy fats, and plenty of fruits and vegetables.

2. **Regular Exercise**: Incorporating physical activity into your routine can enhance your energy and stamina. Aim for at least 150 minutes of moderate aerobic activity each week, including activities you enjoy.

3. **Hydration**: Staying adequately hydrated is crucial for maintaining energy levels. Drink enough water throughout the day, especially if you're active or live in a warm climate.

4. **Quality Sleep**: Aim for 7-9 hours of quality sleep each night. Establish a bedtime routine and create a relaxing sleep environment to improve your sleep quality.

5. **Mindfulness and Stress Management**: Practicing mindfulness, whether through meditation, deep-breathing exercises, or yoga, can help manage stress levels, leading to better energy management throughout the day.

6. **Regular Breaks**: Taking short breaks during your work or study sessions can help maintain focus and prevent fatigue. Consider techniques like the Pomodoro Technique, which involves working for a set period and then taking a short break.

7. **Limit Caffeine and Sugar**: While these can provide a quick boost, they might lead to energy crashes later. Moderation is key.

8. **Routine**: Establishing a consistent daily routine can help regulate your body's natural rhythms, contributing to stable energy levels throughout the day.

Implementing these habits gradually can lead to improved energy and overall well-being. Remember that everyone is different, so it's important to find what works best for you. If you have specific concerns about your energy levels, consider consulting a healthcare professional.

turns.prompt: What's the case for organic produce over conventional?
turns.response:
Choosing organic produce over conventional options can have several potential benefits, which often center around health, flavor, environmental stewardship, and community support. Here are some of the key points:

1. **Absence of Synthetic Pesticides and Fertilizers**: Organic produce is grown without the use of synthetic pesticides and fertilizers, which might reduce exposure to harmful chemicals.

2. **Genetic Modification**: Organic farming typically avoids genetically modified organisms (GMOs), providing an option for those who prefer more natural food sources.

3. **Flavor and Quality**: Many people report that organic fruits and vegetables taste better. This can be attributed to the growing practices that focus on nurturing the soil and plant health.

4. **Environmental Stewardship**: Organic farming practices often promote biodiversity and support healthy ecosystems. By prioritizing natural processes, organic farms may have a lower environmental impact.

5. **Support for Local Communities**: Purchasing organic produce, especially from local farmers, can strengthen local economies and reduce transportation emissions associated with food distribution.

6. **Potential Health Benefits**: While research is ongoing, some studies suggest that organic produce may have higher antioxidant levels compared to conventional options, contributing to better overall health.

Embracing organic produce is often seen as a choice that reflects a commitment to health, quality, and environmental care. However, it's essential to consider your budget and availability when making decisions. If you're uncertain, consulting with a nutritionist or dietitian could provide tailored guidance.

turns.prompt: Are natural supplements generally safe to take?
turns.response:
Natural supplements can be a valuable component of a wellness routine, as they may help address deficiencies and support specific health objectives. However, it's important to note that they should complement a nutritious lifestyle rather than serve as a quick fix.

While many people use natural supplements safely, there are considerations to keep in mind:

1. **Quality and Source**: Not all supplements are created equal, and their quality can vary significantly. It's essential to choose products from reputable brands that undergo rigorous testing.

2. **Interactions**: Natural supplements can interact with medications or other treatments. It's advisable to consult with a healthcare professional, especially if you're taking other medications or have underlying health conditions.

3. **Not a Replacement**: Supplements should not replace a balanced diet and healthy lifestyle choices. They are meant to support, not substitute.

4. **Individual Differences**: Each person's body may respond differently to supplements, depending on various factors such as genetics, health status, and overall diet.

For any specific concerns or symptoms, it's always best to consult a healthcare professional for guidance tailored to your personal health needs.

turns.prompt: Which ones are most commonly recommended?
turns.response:
Commonly recommended natural supplements include:

1. **Multivitamins**: These can help fill nutritional gaps and ensure you're getting essential vitamins and minerals.

2. **Omega-3 Fatty Acids**: Often derived from fish oil, these are beneficial for heart health and may also support cognitive function.

3. **Vitamin D**: Particularly important for bone health, vitamin D can also play a role in immune support.

4. **Probiotics**: These are beneficial bacteria that support gut health and can improve digestive function.

5. **Magnesium**: This mineral is involved in many bodily processes, including muscle and nerve function, and can help with relaxation and sleep.

6. **Turmeric/Curcumin**: Known for its anti-inflammatory properties, turmeric can be beneficial for overall health.

7. **Ashwagandha**: An adaptogen that may help reduce stress and promote overall wellness.

8. **Coenzyme Q10 (CoQ10)**: Often taken for heart health, CoQ10 is involved in energy production at the cellular level.

It's important to remember that the effectiveness and necessity of supplements can vary based on individual health needs. Always consult with a healthcare professional before starting any new supplement regimen.

turns.prompt: What role does socializing play in overall health?
turns.response:
Socializing plays a significant role in overall health by enhancing emotional well-being and combating feelings of loneliness. Engaging with others can help maintain a sense of connection, which is vital for mental health. Here are a few key points regarding the benefits of social interaction:

1. **Emotional Health**: Socializing can nourish emotional health by providing a support system and reducing feelings of isolation. Positive social interactions help to lift mood and increase feelings of happiness.

2. **Mental Stimulation**: Interacting with others also engages the mind, contributing to cognitive health. Conversations and group activities can keep the brain active and sharp.

3. **Meaning and Purpose**: Social connections often contribute to a sense of purpose in life. Being engaged in relationships can make life feel more meaningful.

4. **Resilience Building**: Regular social engagement can build resilience, helping individuals cope better with stress and challenges.

It's important to maintain a balance between social activities and solitary pursuits, as both contribute to overall well-being. Prioritizing time for social engagement alongside individual activities like reading can provide a well-rounded approach to health. 

If you are looking to enhance your social connections, consider reaching out to friends or participating in community events, as these interactions often bring joy and fulfillment.

turns.prompt: What's mindful eating and how do I practice it?
turns.response:
Mindful eating is a practice that involves being fully aware and present during your meals. This means paying attention to the sensory experience of eating—how the food looks, smells, tastes, and feels, as well as recognizing your body’s hunger and fullness signals. It's about fostering a deeper connection with what you consume and recognizing the patterns that influence your eating behaviors.

### How to Practice Mindful Eating

1. **Eat Attentively**: Avoid distractions while eating. Try to sit down at a table instead of eating on the go, and if possible, put away your phone and turn off the TV.

2. **Eat Slowly**: Take your time with each bite. Chew your food thoroughly and pause between bites to assess how you feel. This can help your brain register feelings of fullness more effectively.

3. **Savor Your Food**: Really take the time to enjoy each bite. Notice the flavors, textures, and aromas of your meal. By savoring your food, you can find more pleasure in eating, which may help you feel satisfied with smaller portions.

4. **Tune Into Your Body**: Listen to your body's signals for hunger and fullness. Ask yourself if you are truly hungry or if you are eating for other reasons, such as boredom or stress.

5. **Recognize Your Emotions**: Acknowledge any feelings that arise around food. Rather than dismissing them, consider how these emotions might influence your eating habits.

6. **Create a Calm Environment**: Try to create a peaceful atmosphere during meals. This might involve setting a nice table, using pleasant lighting, or even playing soft background music.

By incorporating these techniques into your eating routine, you may develop a more positive relationship with food, leading to better nutritional choices and improved overall well-being. For further reading, you can check out these resources: [Health Sherpa](https://blog.healthsherpa.com/mindful-eating-practice) and [Mindful.org](https://www.mindful.org/6-ways-practice-mindful-eating).

If you're interested in more specific strategies or guidance, feel free to ask!

result.title: How To Start a Mindful Eating Practice
result.snippet: # How To Start a Mindful Eating Practice. In today’s fast-paced world, where meals are often rushed or consumed on the go, the concept of mindful eating offers a refreshing alternative. Mindful eating is a practice rooted in the centuries-old philosophy of “mindfulness” or being aware of one’s thoughts, emotions, and physical sensations in the present moment. Mindful eating encourages individuals to pay close attention to the sensory experience of eating, including the appearance, taste, texture, and aroma of food. It involves acknowledging the physical sensations of hunger and fullness, as well as recognizing the various factors that influence eating behaviors, such as thoughts, emotions, environmental cues, and social pressures. ## The Benefits of Mindful Eating. By cultivating awareness around eating habits, mindful eating can promote a healthier relationship with food, leading to improved overall well-being. For example, a study published in the Journal of the Academy of Nutrition and Dietetics30077-6/abstract) found that participants who practiced mindful eating reported improvements in dietary intake, eating behaviors, and psychological well-being. ## How to Start Mindful Eating.

result.title: 6 Ways to Practice Mindful Eating
result.snippet: Here are a few simple guidelines to keep in mind to discern between mindless and (more) mindful eating. Eating as mindfully as we do on retreat or in a mindfulness course is not realistic for many of us, especially with families, jobs, and the myriad distractions around us. This is not to mention that our friends, family, and colleagues might not have the patience to eat with us as we take five minutes with each bite. ## What is Mindful Eating? Mindful eating, or conscious eating, is the practice of being fully attentive to your food, your feelings, your hunger, and your satiety cues. Incorporating mindful eating habits into our daily routine isn’t just about eating slower or choosing healthy foods; it’s about nurturing a more intimate and conscious relationship with what we eat. **Ask yourself:** What are some ways you can slow down eating and listen more deeply to your body’s signals?

turns.prompt: How much water should I drink daily?
turns.response:
The amount of water you should drink daily can vary based on several factors, including age, sex, activity level, and climate. Here are some general guidelines:

1. **National Academy of Medicine recommendations**:
   - Men: About **13 cups** (or about 3 liters, which is roughly 101 ounces) of total fluids per day.
   - Women: About **9 cups** (or about 2.2 liters, which is roughly 74 ounces) of total fluids per day.

2. **Common guidelines**: Many individuals follow the "8x8" rule, which suggests drinking **eight 8-ounce glasses** of water a day (about 2 liters or half a gallon). However, this may not be appropriate for everyone.

3. **Additional factors**:
   - If you're physically active, pregnant, or breastfeeding, you might need more fluids.
   - Environmental conditions (like hot weather or high humidity) can also increase your need for water.
   - Remember that other beverages and high-water-content foods (like fruits and vegetables) also contribute to your daily fluid intake.

It's always a good idea to listen to your body and drink when you're thirsty. If you have specific health concerns or conditions, consult with a healthcare professional for personalized recommendations. 

If you'd like, I can help you find more specific resources on hydration!

turns.prompt: What's a good morning routine for wellness?
turns.response:
A good morning routine for wellness can include various elements that promote physical and mental well-being. Here are some suggestions based on daily habits that can shape a healthy life:

1. **Wake Up Early**: Give yourself enough time to start your day without rushing. Early mornings often provide a quiet environment perfect for reflection.

2. **Hydrate**: Drink a glass of water as soon as you wake up. This helps kick-start your metabolism and rehydrates your body after a night's sleep.

3. **Mindfulness or Meditation**: Spend 5-10 minutes engaging in mindfulness practices or meditation. This can help set a positive tone for the day and reduce stress.

4. **Exercise**: Incorporating physical activity, whether it’s stretching, yoga, or a more vigorous workout, can boost your energy levels and improve your mood.

5. **Healthy Breakfast**: Fuel your body with a nutritious breakfast. Include a balance of proteins, healthy fats, and whole grains to keep you energized.

6. **Plan Your Day**: Take a few minutes to jot down your goals for the day. This can help you stay organized and focused.

7. **Gratitude Practice**: Reflect on a few things you are grateful for. This simple practice can enhance your overall outlook.

8. **Avoid Technology Initially**: Try to spend the first part of your morning without checking your phone or email. This helps you start your day with a calm mind.

Remember, the key to an effective morning routine is consistency. Feel free to tweak these suggestions to fit your lifestyle and preferences! Would you like any specific details or additional tips on any part of this routine?

turns.prompt: How does reading regularly benefit mental health?
turns.response:
Reading regularly can significantly benefit mental health in several ways:

1. **Nourishing the Mind**: Reading challenges the brain, enhances cognitive abilities, and strengthens focus. It allows for intellectual engagement and can improve vocabulary and comprehension skills, which contributes to overall cognitive health.

2. **Emotional Fulfillment**: Engaging with stories and characters can evoke various emotions, helping individuals process their feelings and experiences. This can lead to better emotional regulation and a greater understanding of oneself and others.

3. **Reducing Loneliness**: Reading can be a solitary yet fulfilling activity that offers a sense of companionship through characters and narratives. This, in turn, can help combat feelings of loneliness and isolation by providing emotional connection.

4. **Stress Reduction**: Immersing oneself in a book can offer an escape from daily stressors, promoting relaxation and a decrease in anxiety levels. This form of distraction can help improve mood and reduce tension.

5. **Expanding Horizons**: Reading about diverse cultures, experiences, and perspectives fosters empathy and understanding, which can enhance social connections and emotional well-being.

Incorporating regular reading into your routine can be a simple yet powerful way to enhance mental health.

turns.prompt: What's the latest research on intermittent fasting published this year?
turns.response:
Here are some of the latest insights on intermittent fasting based on research published this year:

1. **Weight Loss vs. Health Benefits**: A study from the University of Sydney highlighted that while intermittent fasting can lead to weight loss, it may not offer the same health improvements, such as better insulin resistance and reduced inflammation, as other calorie-restriction diets. The research, published in *Aging Biology*, indicates that the health claims often associated with these diets might be overstated. It emphasizes the importance of consuming nutritious foods during eating periods for maximizing health benefits. [Read more here](https://www.sydney.edu.au/news-opinion/news/2023/02/10/intermittent-fasting-leads-to-weight-loss--not-improved-health.html).

2. **Effectiveness and Safety**: Conversely, research from the University of Illinois Chicago, led by Professor Krista Varady, supports that intermittent fasting, including methods like alternate-day fasting and time-restricted eating, is both effective and safe for weight loss. Participants in her studies experienced average weight reductions between 3% to 8% of their baseline weight. Specifically, a six-hour eating window was noted to help cut calories effectively. [Explore this research](https://ahs.uic.edu/news-stories/research-shows-that-intermittent-fasting-is-safe-and-effective).

These findings suggest that while intermittent fasting can be an effective tool for weight management, attention should also be given to the overall quality of the diet for achieving better health outcomes. If you're considering this approach, it might be valuable to consult with a nutritionist or healthcare provider for personalized guidance.

result.title: Intermittent fasting leads to weight loss not improved health - The University of Sydney
result.snippet: # Intermittent fasting leads to weight loss, not improved health. Intermittent fasting can lead to weight loss, but the foods you do eat should be nutritious to maximise the health benefits of the diet, says Professor Fontana. Intermittent fasting, such as the 5:2 diet has surged in popularity in recent years as a way to lose weight and improve health. However, new research published in the prestigious journal *Aging Biology* has found while these diets involving intermittent fasting could potentially help people lose weight , they lack the beneficial health effects of other forms of weight loss such as improved insulin resistance and reduced inflammation. “There have been a lot of claims that these diets are associated with other benefits alongside weight loss, including increased health and longevity,” says Professor Fontana, the Leonard P. “Early studies on this diet were done in animal models which showed the same pro-longevity benefits we expect of other calorie restrictive diets,” says Professor Fontana, who is also the Scientific Director of the Charles Perkins Centre Royal Prince Alfred Clinic.

result.snippet: # Research shows that intermittent fasting is safe and effective. Intermittent fasting, an increasingly popular weight loss regime, is an effective and safe way to lose weight, according to a body of research from Krista Varady. Varady, a professor of kinesiology and nutrition, has studied different versions of intermittent fasting, such as alternate-day fasting, where people alternate between eating up to 500 calories one day and then whatever they want the next, and time-restricted eating, where people only eat during a fixed window of time each day. Overall, her research has found that intermittent fasting is as good as calorie counting when it comes to weight loss, with people losing, on average, 3% to 8% of baseline weight, depending on the type of fast they’re doing. **A six-hour window of time-restricted eating cuts calories and leads to weight loss:** The researchers followed people who restricted their eating to only four hours a day or to only six hours a day.

turns.prompt: Is creatine FDA approved as a supplement?
turns.response:
Creatine is classified as a dietary supplement rather than a drug, and it is not specifically regulated or approved by the FDA as a supplement. The FDA does not have recommended intake levels for creatine since it falls under the categories defined by the Dietary Supplement Health and Education Act of 1994. This means that while creatine can be sold as a supplement, the FDA does not evaluate it for safety or efficacy before it reaches consumers.

If you're considering using creatine or have specific questions about its effects, it might be beneficial to consult with a healthcare professional or a registered dietitian for personalized advice.

turns.prompt: What's the current WHO guideline on daily sugar intake?
turns.response:
The current World Health Organization (WHO) guidelines recommend that both adults and children reduce their intake of free sugars to less than 10% of their total daily energy intake. For additional health benefits, there's a conditional recommendation to limit free sugars intake to below 5% of total energy intake.

These guidelines aim to help decrease the risk of noncommunicable diseases (NCDs), such as obesity and dental caries, which are significantly associated with higher consumption of free sugars. You can find more information in the WHO's detailed document [here](https://www.who.int/publications/i/item/9789241549028).

The recommendations in this guideline can be used by policy-makers and programme managers to assess current intake levels of free sugars in their countries relative to a benchmark. This guideline should be used in conjunction with other nutrient guidelines and dietary goals, in particular those related to fats and fatty acids (including saturated fatty acids and trans-fatty acids), to guide development of effective public health nutrition policies and programmes to promote a healthy diet. * Information note about intake of sugars recommended in the WHO guideline for adults and children. * News release: WHO calls on countries to reduce sugars intake among adults and children.

result.title: Current WHO recommendation to reduce free sugar intake from all sources to below 10% of daily energy intake for supporting overall health is not well supported by available evidence
result.snippet: # Current WHO recommendation to reduce free sugar intake from all sources to below 10% of daily energy intake for supporting overall health is not well supported by available evidence. In our opinion, the substantial limitations in the current body of evidence, such as short study durations, use of supraphysiological doses of sugar or fructose alone in animal studies, and a lack of direct comparisons of the effects of solid compared with liquid sugars on health outcomes, as well as the lack of appropriate controls, seriously curtail the translatability of the findings to real-world situations. It is quite possible that “high” sugar consumption at normal dietary doses (e.g., 25% daily energy intake) per se—that is, the unique effect of sugar, especially in the solid form—may indeed not pose a health risk for individuals apart from the potential to reduce the overall dietary nutrient density, although newer evidence suggests “low” sugar intake (<5% daily energy intake) is just as likely to be associated with nutrient dilution.
